name: They Laughed When
trigger: Curiosity, underdog social proof, cliffhanger transformation
template: They [laughed/doubted/scoffed] when [I did X] — but when [the turn happened]…
why_it_works: Casts the reader as an ordinary underdog who triumphs, forcing them to learn how.
examples: They Laughed When I Sat Down At The Piano — But When I Started To Play!
They Grinned When the Waiter Spoke to Me in French — But Their Laughter Changed to Amazement at My Reply
They Laughed When I Sent Away for Free Color Film — But Now My Friends Are All Sending Away, Too
My friends mocked my side project — until the first check arrived
Everyone rolled their eyes when I quit — then they saw the numbers

applied_examples.hook: They laughed when I skipped cardio — then I got the leanest I've ever been

name: How To Promise
trigger: Self-interest; a concrete path to a desired result
template: How To [achieve outcome] [without pain / in timeframe]
why_it_works: "How to" implicitly promises useful instruction, the single most reliable opener Caples tested.
examples: How To Win Friends and Influence People
How To Have a Cool, Quiet Bedroom — Even on Hot Nights
How To Do Your Christmas Shopping in 5 Minutes
How To Stop Worrying
How To Get Rid of an Inferiority Complex

applied_examples.hook: How to cut your reporting time in half without hiring an analyst

name: How I Did It
trigger: Curiosity plus relatable first-person proof
template: How I [overcame problem / achieved result] [surprising qualifier]
why_it_works: A personal success story feels like authentic testimony rather than a sales claim.
examples: How I Raised Myself From Failure to Success in Selling
How I Improved My Memory in One Evening
How a Strange Accident Saved Me From Baldness
How $7 Started Me on the Road to $35,000 a Year
How I Made a Fortune With a 'Fool' Idea

name: Who Else Wants
trigger: Bandwagon desire; others already have the result
template: Who Else Wants [desirable result] [without the usual cost]?
why_it_works: "Who else" presupposes others already get the result, triggering me-too desire.
examples: Who Else Wants a Whiter Wash — With No Hard Work?
Who Else Wants a Screen-Star Figure?
Who else wants an extra $1,000 a month without a second job?
Who else wants whiter teeth in one week?
Who else is tired of dieting and wants to eat normally again?

name: These Mistakes
trigger: Fear of hidden, embarrassing failure
template: Do You Make These [number] Mistakes in [domain]?
why_it_works: "These" forces the reader to keep going to find out which mistakes they're committing.
examples: Do You Make These Mistakes in English?
Do You Make These Travel Mistakes?
Do You Do Any of These Ten Embarrassing Things?
Do You Make These 5 Mistakes With Your Resume?
Are you guilty of any of these 9 grammar slip-ups?

applied_examples.hook: Do you make these 4 mistakes when seasoning a steak?

name: Are You Guilty
trigger: Self-reflection; an uncomfortable yes
template: Are You [embarrassed by / missing out on] [topic]?
why_it_works: A direct question pulls the reader into self-diagnosis, and the answer feels personal.
examples: Are You Ashamed of the Smells in Your Home?
Is the Life of a Child Worth $1 to You?
Can You Talk About Books With the Rest of Them?
Are you quietly overpaying for insurance?
Are you the last person still doing it the hard way?

name: Announcing / Introducing
trigger: Novelty; fear of missing what's new
template: Announcing [new thing] that [delivers benefit]
why_it_works: Caples found news headlines among the highest-pulling because people crave what's new.
examples: Announcing the New Ford Cars
Introducing the New Anti-Aging Cream
Presenting the Most Comfortable Chair Ever Engineered
New … a Cream Deodorant Which Safely Stops Perspiration
Announcing a faster way to do your taxes

name: Now / At Last
trigger: Anticipation resolved; the wait is over
template: Now [you can have X] / At Last, [long-awaited solution] is here
why_it_works: "Now" and "At Last" frame the offer as overdue relief that has finally arrived.
examples: Now! Own Florida Land the Easy Way — $20 Down and $20 a Month
At Last — a Shampoo That Won't Dry Your Hair
Now Any Auto Repair Job Can Be 'Duck Soup' for You
Finally — relief from late-night heartburn
At last, a budgeting app that doesn't make you feel guilty

name: The Warning
trigger: Loss aversion; threat avoidance
template: Warning: [bad outcome] if you [common action] / before you [act]
why_it_works: "Warning" is a pattern-interrupt that triggers protective attention faster than benefits do.
examples: Warning: Don't Buy a Car Until You Read This
Warning to Husbands Who Want a Happy Marriage
Caution: Are You Killing Your Best Salesman?
Warning: don't sign that lease until you read this
Read this before you hire your next employee

applied_examples.hook: Warning: don't renew that contract until you check these 3 clauses

name: To People Who
trigger: Self-identification; "this is for someone like me"
template: To [specific group] Who Want [specific outcome]
why_it_works: Naming the exact audience makes the right reader feel the message was written only for them.
examples: To Men Who Want To Quit Work Some Day
To the Man Who Is 35 and Dissatisfied
To People Who Want To Write — But Can't Get Started
To a $15,000 Man Who Would Like To Be Making $30,000
To busy parents who never have time to cook

name: Specific-Detail Proof
trigger: Credibility through one vivid, concrete fact
template: At [specific measure], [surprising specific observation about product]
why_it_works: A precise, almost trivial detail proves quality more persuasively than any superlative.
examples: At 60 Miles an Hour the Loudest Noise in This New Rolls-Royce Comes From the Electric Clock
This pan holds heat for exactly 11 minutes after the burner's off
Our battery survives 1,000 charge cycles — we counted
It takes 4 seconds to boot. We timed it.
1,000,000 People Bought Schick This Year

name: Give Me X and I'll
trigger: Low-risk reciprocity; small ask, big payoff
template: Give Me [small time / resource] and I'll [deliver big result]
why_it_works: Quantifying a tiny investment against a large reward makes the deal feel irresistibly fair.
examples: Give Me 5 Days and I'll Give You a Magnetic Personality
Give Me 5 Minutes and I'll Make You a Better Public Speaker
Give Me 15 Minutes and I'll Give You the Secret of Learning Any Subject
Give me one weekend and I'll fix your sleep
Give me 10 minutes and I'll save you $500 on your phone bill

name: Guarantee / Risk-Reversal
trigger: Removes fear of loss; reverses the risk
template: [Result] or [your money back / I'll do X]
why_it_works: Putting the seller's promise on the line transfers risk away from the buyer.
examples: Double Your Money Back If This Isn't the Best Onion Soup You Ever Tasted
Play Guitar in 7 Days or Money Back
The Most Comfortable Shoes You've Ever Worn — Or Your Money Back
Lose 10 pounds in 30 days or it's free
Rank on page one in 90 days or you don't pay

name: Thousands Now
trigger: Bandwagon; if thousands did it, so can I
template: [Thousands / Number] Now [do desirable thing] Who Never Thought They Could
why_it_works: Mass adoption proves the result is achievable by ordinary people — including the reader.
examples: Thousands Now Play Who Never Thought They Could
Thousands Have This Priceless Gift — But Never Discover It
1,000,000 People Bought Schick This Year
Thousands now wake up rested who used to fight their alarm
Thousands are speaking Spanish who failed it in school

name: The Why Reason
trigger: Curiosity about a counterintuitive cause
template: Why [surprising or undesirable thing] [happens / is true]
why_it_works: "Why" promises an explanation for something the reader didn't realize needed explaining.
examples: Why Some People Almost Always Make Money in the Stock Market
Why Men Crack
Why Some Foods 'Explode' in Your Stomach
Why your most productive hour is the one you waste
Why expensive coffee tastes worse to most people

name: Customer Quote
trigger: Believability through a real customer's voice
template: "[Customer's spoken claim about the result]"
why_it_works: Copy in the customer's own words outperforms advertiser claims; quotes signal authentic testimony.
examples: "Last Friday … Was I Scared! — My Boss Almost Fired Me"
"I Was a 97-Pound Weakling"
"I never thought I'd say this about a spreadsheet, but I love it."
"When Doctors 'Feel Rotten' This Is What They Do"
"I cancelled my gym the day I tried this."

name: The Secret Of
trigger: Implied insider knowledge you don't possess
template: The [little-known] secret of [result] — without [common cost]
why_it_works: "Secret" presupposes a withheld answer, opening a curiosity loop only reading can close.
examples: The incredibly simple secret of making the same amount of food seem TWICE AS MUCH
The secret to negotiating like a pro, even if you're not a born salesperson
The little-known secret that can make any new copywriter drown in money
The secret to skin that glows — without expensive products
The closely-guarded secret of restaurants' best sauces

name: Little-Known Way
trigger: Scarcity of knowledge plus low effort
template: The little-known [way/trick] to [benefit] that [most people] never use
why_it_works: Pairs exclusivity with an implied shortcut, signaling rare leverage the reader is missing.
examples: Little-known ways to write fascinating bullet points
A little-known way to cut your grocery bill in half
The little-known stretch physical therapists use to kill back pain in 60 seconds
An overlooked tax move that puts thousands back in your pocket
The little-known setting that doubles your phone's battery life

name: It's Not What You Think
trigger: Subverted expectation; pattern-interrupt
template: Why [outcome] — and it's not [the obvious cause]
why_it_works: Naming and dismissing the reader's first guess proves a deeper answer exists.
examples: Why your diet keeps failing — and no, it's not your willpower
The real reason your team misses deadlines (hint: it's not laziness)
Why you wake up tired even after 8 hours — it's not the sleep
Magic exists (but it's not what you think it is)
Why your ads stopped working — it has nothing to do with budget

name: What Never To Do
trigger: Loss aversion; fear of an unknown error
template: What never to [common action] — never. And how to [avoid the harm]
why_it_works: An emphatic prohibition implies the reader may already be making a costly mistake.
examples: What never to keep in your safe-deposit box. Never.
What never to say in a salary negotiation
The one food you should never eat before a flight
What never to do in the first 60 seconds of a sales call
Never do this on a first cold email — it kills your reply rate

name: Warning Signs
trigger: Self-diagnosis anxiety; "is this me?"
template: Do you recognize the [number] warning signs of [problem]?
why_it_works: A symptom checklist pulls the reader into self-examination to find out if they're at risk.
examples: Do You Recognize the 7 Early Warning Signs of High Blood Pressure?
Do you recognize the 5 silent signs your best people are about to quit?
Do you recognize these 6 signs your savings won't last?
5 warning signs your website is quietly losing customers
Do you recognize the 3 early signs of burnout?

name: Which Of These
trigger: Forced self-selection; personal relevance
template: Which of these [number] [problems/mistakes] is [costing] you [X]?
why_it_works: A multiple-choice frame compels the reader to pick their answer, resolvable only in the copy.
examples: Which of These 5 Money-Making Skills Are You Ignoring?
Which of these mistakes is quietly killing your open rates?
Which of these 7 hidden fees is draining your account?
Which of these common foods is sabotaging your sleep?
Which of these little-known tax breaks do you qualify for?

name: Where To Find
trigger: Promise of a discoverable, locatable resource
template: Where to find [scarce/valuable thing] — even if [obstacle]
why_it_works: Implies a concrete, findable destination the reader lacks, turning desire into a treasure hunt.
examples: Where to Find Clients Who'll Happily Pay Triple Your Rate
Where to find genuine antiques for a song
Where to find the best-paying remote jobs nobody posts publicly
Where to find money for your startup when banks say no
Where to find the cheapest flights — the sites airlines hope you skip

name: If You Can X, You Can Y
trigger: Self-qualification; personalized payoff for a low bar
template: If you [small thing], you can [outsized benefit]
why_it_works: The "if" lets readers qualify in, and the "then" promises disproportionate reward for low effort.
examples: If You Can Read, You Can Learn to Draw in 30 Days
If you can spare 10 minutes a morning, you can rewire your focus
If you own a home, you may be owed a tax refund you never claimed
If you can send a text, you can build your first website tonight
If you hate networking, here's how to land clients without it

name: Truth They Hide
trigger: Conspiracy of silence; forbidden knowledge
template: The truth about [topic] that [authority/industry] doesn't want you to know
why_it_works: Framing information as deliberately hidden triggers reactance and a pull to see for yourself.
examples: The truth about cholesterol your doctor was never taught
What the supplement industry doesn't want you to know about 'all-natural'
The truth about credit scores the banks profit from you not knowing
What never makes it into the brochure: the truth about timeshares
What your favorite guru isn't telling you about success

name: The One Thing
trigger: Cognitive ease; the single highest-leverage answer
template: The one [thing/word/move] that [makes or breaks the outcome] — and almost everyone gets it wrong
why_it_works: Reducing a complex problem to one decisive factor promises clarity and implies the reader errs too.
examples: The one word that doubled my reply rate on cold emails
The single biggest mistake people make with their 401(k)
The one question to ask before you hire anyone
The one habit that separates six-figure freelancers from everyone else
The one ingredient to cut from your diet this week

name: Ordinary Person, Extraordinary Result
trigger: Identification; "if they can, I can"
template: How a [ordinary person with limitation] [achieved extraordinary result] in [timeframe]
why_it_works: A relatable underdog makes the result feel replicable and opens a "how did they do it?" loop.
examples: How a broke college dropout built a $40k/month business from his dorm
How a 58-year-old grandmother ran her first marathon with no athletic background
How a small-town teacher paid off $90,000 in debt in two years
How an ex-waitress out-negotiated three Wall Street recruiters
How a self-taught coder landed a six-figure job in 11 months

name: Pleasant Surprise Bonus
trigger: Reward-prediction error; delight
template: [Unexpected benefit from a simple action] — and [bonus second benefit]
why_it_works: An unexpected double payoff feels like found money and implies the offer keeps over-delivering.
examples: This 5-minute morning routine sharpens focus — and quietly lowers blood pressure too
Switch to this one app and cut your bills — plus reclaim three hours a week
Read this before bed to sleep deeper (and wake with clearer skin)
Eating one banana a day may make you more attractive (two might help you live longer)
Walk 20 minutes daily to lose weight — and double your creative output

name: Right? Wrong!
trigger: Challenged belief; cognitive dissonance
template: You think [common belief], right? Wrong. Here's what actually [works]
why_it_works: Stating the reader's own assumption then negating it creates dissonance demanding resolution.
examples: You should post daily to grow, right? Wrong!
More protein builds more muscle, right? Wrong — here's the cap nobody mentions
Cardio is the fastest way to lose fat? Wrong.
You need a big email list to make money. Wrong — small and loyal beats big and cold
Lowering your price wins more clients, right? It does the opposite

name: Naked Benefit, Hidden Mechanism
trigger: Desire for the result plus curiosity about the hidden how
template: [Vivid end-benefit stated as fact] — using [intriguing unnamed mechanism], on page [X]
why_it_works: State the dream outcome plainly to create want, then withhold the mechanism so they must read on.
examples: On page 33, a six-second motion with your mouth that instantly relaxes your chest and heart
The single page in this report that could erase your back pain by Sunday
A 12-word email that gets billionaires to write back — it's in Chapter 4
Make anybody like you from the very first moment they meet you
The two-minute habit that wipes out afternoon brain fog

name: Problem Callout
trigger: Self-recognition; people attend to their own pain
template: Still [struggling with PROBLEM] every [TIME PERIOD]?
why_it_works: Naming the reader's exact frustration makes them feel seen, so they keep reading.
examples: Still losing an hour every morning hunting for your keys?
Frustrated with a job that drains you by Tuesday?
Tired of pouring money into ads that go nowhere?
Still copy-pasting the same email ten times a day?
Worried about the safety of your family while you sleep?

name: Agitation Twist
trigger: Loss aversion; the unspoken cost made vivid
template: And the worst part? Every [TIME UNIT] you wait, [consequence worsens]
why_it_works: Intensifying the pain before relief raises the perceived value of the solution.
examples: And the worst part? Every month you wait, that interest quietly doubles
Chronic stress doesn't stay still — it becomes insomnia, then headaches, then worse
That clutter isn't shrinking. It's multiplying while you're not looking
Every day you delay, a competitor ships the feature your users wanted
The longer you ignore it, the more it costs to fix

name: Before-After Bridge
trigger: Contrast; transformation makes the gap tangible
template: Before: [painful status quo]. After: [desired outcome].
why_it_works: Showing the reader's happy ending first creates emotional pull toward the means of getting there.
examples: Before: 10 hours a week on manual reports. After: it runs while you sleep
Last year she couldn't run a mile. This morning she finished her first marathon
You used to dread Mondays. Imagine looking forward to them
From 300 forgotten passwords to remembering exactly one
Before: inbox chaos. After: zero unread by noon

name: Slippery First Line
trigger: Frictionless momentum; nothing to resist
template: [2–4 word fragment].
why_it_works: A short, almost-incomplete first line is so easy to read the reader slides into the next sentence.
examples: It's easy.
Picture this.
Let me explain.
Here's the deal.
I goofed.
Read on.
source: Joseph Sugarman, The Adweek Copywriting Handbook (thisiscopy.com)

name: Seed of Curiosity
trigger: Open loop; the brain demands closure
template: [Statement]. But [there's more / wait until you hear this].
why_it_works: Ending on an unresolved promise forces the reader onto the next line for the payoff.
examples: But there's more.
And that's not even the strange part.
What happened next surprised even me.
So read on — this is where it gets interesting.
I almost left out the most important part.

name: Non-Sequitur Open
trigger: Surprise; an unexpected line demands explanation
template: [Odd or seemingly unrelated statement]. (You'll see why in a second.)
why_it_works: A line that seemingly has nothing to do with the offer creates a gap the reader must resolve.
examples: Why are the wheels crooked?
There's a reason I keep a brick on my desk.
This has nothing to do with money. At first.
My horse is laughing all the way to the bank.
I almost didn't write this.

name: Dear Friend Callout
trigger: Belonging; self-selection of the right reader
template: Dear [specific person/group]: here's something [you've wanted].
why_it_works: Speaking to a precisely named reader makes the message feel personally addressed, not broadcast.
examples: Dear Friend, here's something that could change how you work forever
To every founder who's tired of guessing: this is for you
If you're a parent of a teenager, read this twice
This letter is for people who refuse to retire broke
Attention, anyone who's ever been burned by a contractor

name: Attention Hail
trigger: Reflexive response to being summoned
template: Attention [specific audience]: [bold promise/warning]
why_it_works: A direct hail filters for the right reader and triggers the instinct to respond when called.
examples: Attention small business owners: your tax deadline just moved
Calling all night-shift workers who can't fall asleep
Homeowners: this one form could save you $4,000
Freelancers — stop sending invoices the old way
Hey coaches who are tired of trading time for money

name: Ultra-Specific Number
trigger: Credibility; precise numbers read as truth
template: How I [specific result: number] in [specific timeframe]
why_it_works: Exact figures feel verifiable and earned, while round numbers feel guessed — specificity buys trust.
examples: How I cut my grocery bill by $237 in 30 days
The 11-minute routine that replaced my gym membership
We increased their leads 30% in 90 days — here's the playbook
5,768 owners switched last quarter. Here's why.
7 words that doubled this page's reply rate

name: Closing Window
trigger: Scarcity; time pressure forces action
template: [Action/outcome] before [deadline / it's gone]
why_it_works: A closing window converts passive interest into immediate reading and action.
examples: Lock in last year's rate before Friday
Read this before the rules change next month
Only the next 48 hours decide your spot
The window for this closes Sunday at midnight
Get in before the price goes up tonight

name: Imagine
trigger: Mental rehearsal; imagined ownership feels real
template: Imagine [desired future state], without [usual obstacle]
why_it_works: Getting the reader to picture the outcome lets them pre-experience the benefit, raising desire.
examples: Imagine closing your laptop at 4pm with everything done
Picture an inbox that empties itself by lunch
Imagine never rewriting the same email twice
Picture this: zero spreadsheets, total clarity
Imagine waking up before your alarm, actually rested

name: What If
trigger: Curiosity plus hope; opens a desirable possibility
template: What if [hard thing] were actually [easy/possible]?
why_it_works: A hypothetical reframes the reader's assumed limits and motivates them to read for the proof.
examples: What if you never had to chase a late payment again?
What if learning a language took 15 minutes a day?
What if your slowest month became your best?
What if the problem isn't you — it's your tools?
What if you could write a week of content in one hour?

name: Confession
trigger: Intimacy plus vulnerability signals honesty
template: I'm about to admit something [embarrassing/risky]: [confession]
why_it_works: A vulnerable admission creates instant trust and a curiosity gap about what comes next.
examples: I almost didn't write this. What I'm about to admit is embarrassing.
I wasted three years doing it the wrong way. Here's what finally worked.
Confession: I used to charge half what I was worth.
I'm about to tell you something my competitors would hate.
I lied on my first sales call. It taught me everything.

name: In Medias Res Scene
trigger: Narrative tension; a mid-action scene demands resolution
template: It was [time], and I was [in a tense moment] when [turning point]
why_it_works: Dropping the reader into live conflict, not setup, sinks a hook only the next sentences release.
examples: It was 2 a.m. in an empty parking garage when I finally understood why nothing worked
The phone rang at the exact moment I decided to quit
Three minutes before the pitch, the slides went blank
She was halfway out the door when she saw the number on the check
The doctor looked at me and said three words I'll never forget

name: The Stop Command
trigger: Loss aversion plus pattern interrupt; you're harming yourself
template: Stop [common action]. It's making your [outcome] worse.
why_it_works: Reframes a trusted habit as the actual cause of the reader's problem, forcing a re-read.
examples: Stop writing more content. It's burying your best work.
Stop doing 100 crunches a day — here's the one move that works
Stop optimizing your funnel. Fix this first.
Stop networking. Start doing this instead.
Stop apologizing in your emails. It's costing you deals.

name: Nobody Talks About This
trigger: Forbidden / insider knowledge; scarcity of information
template: Nobody talks about [X]... but it's the real reason [problem/outcome]
why_it_works: Promises information the reader's peers don't have, framing it as exclusive and consequential.
examples: Nobody talks about THIS, but it's the real reason your launch flopped
This is the one thing nobody tells you about freelancing
Nobody told me this when I started. Now I'm telling you.
There's a reason nobody talks about this — and it's costing you money
Nobody warns you about the second year of running a business

name: Unpopular Opinion
trigger: Tribal signaling and debate; tension demands a side
template: Unpopular opinion: [hot take] — and I stand by it
why_it_works: Flagging a take as unpopular invites the reader to take a side, driving comments and shares.
examples: Unpopular opinion: most A/B tests are a waste of time
Unpopular opinion: your morning routine doesn't matter
Hot take: cold email isn't dead, your offer is
I'll probably get hate for this, but most courses are overpriced
Controversial: hustle culture is making you slower

name: Everything You Know Is Wrong
trigger: Cognitive dissonance; threat to existing belief
template: Everything you've been told about [topic] is [wrong / debunked]
why_it_works: Attacks the reader's entire mental model at once, making them read to defend or update it.
examples: Everything you've been told about productivity is based on a debunked 1950s study
The biggest lie in marketing isn't what you think
Forget everything you think you know about SEO
What if everything you've been taught about saving money is wrong?
Most of what you learned about dieting is outdated

name: The Real Reason
trigger: Open loop; hidden true cause behind a visible symptom
template: The real reason [you have problem] isn't [obvious cause]. It's [withheld].
why_it_works: Dismisses the obvious explanation and dangles the true one, opening a gap the reader must close.
examples: The real reason your emails don't get opened has nothing to do with your subject line
The real reason you're tired all the time isn't sleep
Here's the real reason your ads stopped converting
The harder you try to grow, the slower it gets. Here's the real reason why.
The real reason customers churn (and it's not price)

name: POV
trigger: Self-recognition; the reader sees their exact moment mirrored
template: POV: you [hyper-specific relatable situation]
why_it_works: Drops the reader inside a precise scenario, triggering 'that's literally me' and an urge to share.
examples: POV: you finally finished the project at 2am and exported the wrong file
POV: the client wants 'one small change'
POV: you open your laptop to do one thing and lose an hour
POV: it's Sunday night and the Monday dread just hit
POV: your code worked yesterday and you changed nothing

name: Without Telling Me
trigger: In-group identity play; participatory humor
template: Tell me you're [a type] without telling me you're [that type]
why_it_works: Turns identity into a game, inviting the reader to supply their own punchline.
examples: Tell me you're a freelancer without telling me you're a freelancer
Tell me you've worked in retail without telling me you've worked in retail
Tell me your startup is pre-revenue without telling me it's pre-revenue
Tell me you're an only child without telling me you're an only child
Tell me you skipped breakfast without telling me you skipped breakfast

name: Social Proof at Scale
trigger: Bandwagon; if this many chose it, it must be right
template: [Big number] [people/users] [did X]. Here's why.
why_it_works: A large, specific count borrows the crowd's credibility and frames the reader as the outlier.
examples: 10,000 founders use this in their first 90 days. Here's why.
Join 130,000 marketers who read this every week
200+ people asked me about this, so I'm finally writing it down
8 out of 10 writers quit in year one. The other 2 do this.
Over 1 million downloads can't all be wrong

name: I Did It So You Don't Have To
trigger: Vulnerability builds trust; reciprocity for saved effort
template: I [wasted/spent] [time/money] on [X] so you don't have to. Here's what I learned.
why_it_works: A costly admission signals honesty and frames the content as a gift that spares the reader the pain.
examples: I spent $23,847 testing 147 landing pages so you don't have to
I wasted 3 years building the wrong product. Here are the 5 signs I ignored.
I read 52 business books last year. Only 4 mattered.
I tried every productivity app so you can skip 40 of them
I made every cold-email mistake. Steal the version that finally worked.

name: I Went From X To Y
trigger: Aspiration plus proof; if they did it, so can I
template: I went from [bad starting state] to [desirable end state] in [timeframe]. Here's how.
why_it_works: A concrete contrast makes the result feel credible and the reader self-projects into the after-state.
examples: I went from 0 to 10,000 subscribers in 6 months with one channel
From 80-hour weeks to a 4-day workweek in 18 months
Last year: $4.7M. This year, after one change: $11.2M.
I went from terrified of public speaking to keynoting in a year
From maxed-out credit cards to debt-free in 24 months

name: FOMO Clock
trigger: Quantified cost of inaction; time is running out now
template: By the time you finish reading this, [cost / loss / change] will have happened
why_it_works: Attaches a ticking, real-time consequence to reading, reframing procrastination as immediate loss.
examples: By the time you finish reading this, 40 competitors will have shipped
Every day you wait costs you roughly $300. Here's the math.
By the time most founders realize this, it's already too late
In the 30 seconds it takes to read this, you'll lose three potential leads
Read this before you launch — you'll thank me later

name: Numbered List
trigger: Cognitive ease; finite, scannable, low-commitment promise
template: [Number] [ways/mistakes/lessons] to [achieve outcome] (without [pain])
why_it_works: A specific number sets a clear, bounded expectation and signals quick, organized value.
examples: 7 ways to write faster without sacrificing quality
21 cooking hacks that will change your life
5 lessons from losing my first $100k
9 emails that doubled our reply rate (steal them)
3 tools that replaced my entire marketing stack

name: This vs That
trigger: Contrast resolves ambiguity; helps the reader pick a side
template: [Option X] vs [Option Y]: [the one most people get wrong]
why_it_works: A head-to-head frame implies a verdict the reader wants, and contrast makes value instantly legible.
examples: Cold email vs. cold DM: which actually books meetings?
Hiring vs. outsourcing — the math nobody runs
Notion vs. a notebook: I tried both for 30 days
SEO vs. paid ads: where your first $1,000 should go
Buying vs. renting: the number that changes everything

name: Index-Card Mechanism
trigger: Specific promise plus method; "here's exactly how"
template: How to [specific result] in [timeframe] (even if [objection])
why_it_works: Pairs a concrete outcome with a clear path and pre-handles the reader's main excuse for failing.
examples: How to make $1,000/month from your blogging hobby
How to write a landing page in 90 minutes, even if you hate writing
Here's exactly how to land your first 10 clients in 30 days
The entire strategy fits on an index card — and it generated $8M
How to learn any skill 2x faster (even with a full-time job)

name: Diagnostic Question
trigger: Self-assessment loop; an unanswered question nags
template: When was the last time you [desirable action/feeling]?
why_it_works: A pointed question forces the reader to mentally answer, and the answer pulls them into the copy.
examples: When was the last time you opened your analytics and felt genuinely excited?
How much revenue are you leaving on the table every month?
What would change if your email list doubled in 90 days?
When did you last finish a workday actually finished?
How many hours a week do you lose to tasks a robot could do?
